course_subtitle: Enhanced telemetry for finding what failed, where, and why in AI optical fabrics.
index_footer_line: OCP Networking Project's Optics Reliability Workstream: Enhanced Telemetry Spec - 2026

term_glossary.tooltip: Physical-media-dependent implementation details for the optical module path.
Common Management Interface Specification used for pluggable transceiver management.
Two-wire local management interface used by the host to communicate with the local transceiver.
Versatile Diagnostics Monitoring pages in CMIS used by many module telemetry values.
Signal-to-noise ratio; a signal quality measurement used for host and line lanes.
Forward error correction; error-correction behavior used to understand link stress and uncorrectable failures.
Bit error rate; the ratio of errored bits to total transmitted bits, used to quantify link quality.
Digital diagnostics monitoring interface values and warning or alarm flags reported by the module.
Command Data Block; a CMIS command-reply mechanism used for telemetry collection and transfer.

modules.title: The AI Fabric Visibility Problem
modules.subtitle: Enhanced telemetry as an operations model for local and remote optical endpoints.

slides.title: The AI Fabric Visibility Problem
slides.subtitle: Enhanced telemetry for finding optical link issues across local and remote endpoints.

slides.title: The Reliability Questions This Course Answers
slides.subtitle: Four modules move from the operational problem to deployable host workflows.
modules_listed: CDB Workflows, Event Logs, and Remote Access
Firmware, Host Software, and Deployment Readiness

chips.text: OCP Community volunteers from Credo and Oracle

slides.title: Link Flaps Become Fabric-Scale Events
slides.subtitle: Large AI fabrics make optical blind spots expensive because one local symptom can have a remote cause.

bullets.text: Fabrics connect many accelerators, switches, optics, and server endpoints, so one physical symptom may cross several ownership boundaries.
Bare-metal or remote endpoints can hide the far-side optical module from the operator who sees the switch alarm.
A link flap can interrupt training, qualification, or service workflows that depend on predictable fabric health.
Operators need consistent telemetry across vendors and form factors before they can compare symptoms at scale.

slides.title: The Three-Part Software Stack
slides.subtitle: The spec separates the system into transceiver implementation, host software, and telemetry-server integration.

bullets.text: The module gathers local and remote telemetry, maintains event history, and exposes common command behavior.
The host polls modules, requests local or remote data, and prepares operational output through SDK or command-line tools.
A server can compile and summarize overall network health, but its implementation remains user dependent and outside the spec scope.

figure.alt: Three-part telemetry software stack from transceiver firmware to host software to telemetry server.
figure.caption: The specification focuses on transceiver behavior and the common interface to host software; telemetry-server design is intentionally left to operators.

slides.title: What the Specification Includes and Excludes
slides.subtitle: The contribution targets common telemetry behavior without trying to own every monitoring system above it.

bullets.text: The spec applies to 400G, 800G, and 1.6T compliant transceivers.
The telemetry model is intended to be independent of the module physical-media-dependent implementation.
The spec uses OIF CMIS 5.x functions and adds definitions and modifications for enhanced telemetry.
Telemetry-server implementation is user dependent and not within the scope of the document.

slides.title: Six Functions of a Compliant Transceiver
slides.subtitle: The specification turns raw module data into a common visibility model for operations.

cards.title: Mission-mode monitoring
cards.body: Collect statistics on host-facing and line-facing receivers while the link is in service.

cards.title: Link-health reporting
cards.body: Track and log telemetry through a common health metric rather than isolated counters.

cards.title: Remote identification
cards.body: Use in-band communication to exchange data between the transceivers at both ends of a link.

cards.title: Event logging
cards.body: Notify, timestamp, and preserve important events in non-volatile module memory.

cards.title: Hitless firmware update
cards.body: Support local and remote firmware update without requiring service disruption as the operating goal.

cards.title: Host harvesting
cards.body: Enable switch software to collect telemetry and prepare it for streaming to a telemetry server.

slides.title: A Link Is More Than One Fiber Path

figure.alt: Silent animation showing switch electrical, optical, and host electrical link segments with telemetry localization.
figure.caption: Segment-level telemetry separates the switch-module electrical link, the optical path, and the host-module electrical link into independently monitored evidence.

slides.title: Breakout Links Multiply Remote Endpoints

figure.alt: Breakout optical link example where one switch module connects to multiple host modules.
figure.caption: In breakout or shuffle use cases, one switch port may need independent telemetry from more than one remote partner.

slides.title: Visibility Starts With a Shared Model
items: The operational problem is finding the failing segment, not just noticing that a link changed state.
The standardized boundary is transceiver behavior and the common interface to host software.
Compliant optics are expected to support local and remote telemetry collection.
Breakout links make endpoint identity and independent remote monitoring essential.
Telemetry-server design remains operator dependent, but the module-to-host model can be common.

slides.title: Check Your Model: Visibility and Scope
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: What is the primary visibility gap enhanced optics telemetry is meant to close?

choices.text: A local link alarm may not reveal which local, optical, or remote segment caused the problem.
Telemetry servers need one mandated implementation for every operator.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The spec leaves telemetry-server implementation user dependent. The common work is at the transceiver and host interface.

choices.text: Every optical module needs the same physical-media-dependent design.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The scope is intended to be PMD independent, so a common telemetry model can apply across implementations.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The value is local and remote evidence that maps symptoms to the failing segment.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the visibility model: the goal is to connect local symptoms with local, optical, and remote endpoint evidence.

questions.prompt: Which functions belong in the compliant transceiver and host telemetry model?

choices.text: Mission-mode link monitoring and statistics collection.
In-band remote identification and data exchange between link partners.
Programmable event notification and non-volatile event logging.
A required telemetry-server product architecture.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The shared model includes module and host functions, while server implementation remains open.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Keep the boundary clear: compliant module behavior and host harvesting are standardized; the telemetry-server implementation is not prescribed.

slides.title: Up Next: Metrics and Link Health
slides.thank_you_message: Nice work - you have finished the visibility model for OCP optics reliability.

modules.title: Metrics, Flags, and Link Health
modules.subtitle: Telemetry categories, summary flags, masks, and the worst-category link-health score.

slides.title: Metrics, Flags, and Link Health
slides.subtitle: How compliant optics turn raw measurements into actionable link state.

slides.title: Signals You Will Interpret
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to classify telemetry and explain how link health is summarized.
objectives: Distinguish local and remote telemetry queries at a conceptual level.
Classify common metrics into quality, error, recovery, environmental, and context categories.
Explain why the link-health score is driven by the weakest category rather than an average.
Describe how flags and masks help operators focus monitoring behavior.

slides.title: Local and Remote Queries Use One Mental Model
slides.subtitle: The host asks the local module; the local module may return its own data or retrieve partner data in-band.

bullets.text: The host requests telemetry from the module it can reach directly over I2C.
The local module retrieves telemetry from a remote link partner and presents it back to the same host.
Remote requests may need to identify which partner or port is being queried.

figure.alt: Host queries local module over I2C and local module reaches remote partner in-band.
figure.caption: A consistent host workflow can still distinguish local data, remote data, and breakout partner identity.

slides.title: Status Pages Establish the Baseline
slides.subtitle: The module exposes standard CMIS status, configuration, VDM, CDB, payload, and vendor areas at a high level.

bullets.text: Common module pages provide baseline identity, status, and control information.
Standard pages establish operating state before enhanced telemetry is interpreted.
Many measured values align with CMIS VDM concepts and are stored for host access.
Command and payload areas support telemetry retrieval, event logs, and larger replies.
Vendor-specific areas may still exist, but the course focuses on the common operational interface.

slides.title: Optical and Electrical Quality Metrics
slides.subtitle: Quality measurements help separate line-side optical issues from host-side electrical issues.

cards.title: SNR
cards.body: Signal-to-noise ratio on optical line lanes and electrical host lanes.

cards.title: Eye height
cards.body: Host-side and line-side eye-height measurements indicate signal margin.

cards.title: PAM4 LTP
cards.body: Level transition parameters help characterize host and line PAM4 signal behavior.

cards.title: MPI estimation
cards.body: Multipath interference estimates help identify optical reflection or path effects.

cards.title: Host vs line
cards.body: Separating host-facing and line-facing values helps localize the weak segment.

cards.title: Per-lane view
cards.body: Lane-level values prevent one degraded lane from disappearing inside a port-level summary.

slides.title: Error and Recovery Metrics
slides.subtitle: Error counters and recovery behavior show whether a link is merely noisy or actually losing service.

cards.title: Pre-FEC BER
cards.body: Bit error behavior before correction shows raw link stress.

cards.title: FEC histogram
cards.body: Accumulated errors per FEC frame help characterize correction load.

cards.title: Uncorrectable codewords
cards.body: Failures that correction cannot fix point to more serious link degradation.

cards.title: Relinks
cards.body: Link drop and reacquisition counters show instability over time.

cards.title: LOS and LOL
cards.body: Loss-of-signal and loss-of-link events identify state transitions that need history.

cards.title: Uptime
cards.body: Elapsed time since the last link-up gives recovery and stability context.

slides.title: Environment and Module Context
slides.subtitle: Temperature, power, serial identity, and warnings turn measurements into traceable evidence.

cards.title: Module temperature
cards.body: Module temperature indicates whether thermal conditions may contribute to link quality.

cards.title: Laser temperature
cards.body: Laser temperature provides optical-device context for drift and alarm behavior.

cards.title: Voltage
cards.body: Supply context helps correlate link issues with local electrical conditions.

cards.title: Rx power
cards.body: Received optical power helps separate optical path loss from other symptoms.

cards.title: DDMI warnings
cards.body: Alarm and warning flags expose module-reported threshold crossings.

cards.title: Serial number
cards.body: Identity fields tie telemetry and event history to the physical module.

source_refs.section: Module Telemetry Data and Telemetry Info

slides.title: Link Health Score Uses the Worst Category

figure.alt: Silent animation showing multiple link-health category scores where the reported health follows the weakest category.
figure.caption: The minimum-category rule keeps one bad field from being masked by healthier measurements elsewhere.

slides.title: Flags and Masks Keep Monitoring Actionable
slides.subtitle: Summary flags make state visible; masks let deployments control which counters influence the score.

bullets.text: Flags can show whether metric groups have crossed thresholds or need operator attention.
Masks can include or exclude selected counters from link-health-score calculations.
A deployment can reduce noise without losing the shared metric model.

figure.alt: Diagram showing flag summary feeding mask controls for link health.
figure.caption: Flags and masks help monitoring remain common enough to compare and flexible enough to operate.

source_refs.section: Telemetry Flag Summary Reporting and Custom Mask

slides.title: Metrics Should Point to Action
items: Local and remote telemetry use the same host-centered operating model.
Quality metrics separate host-side electrical behavior from line-side optical behavior.
Error, recovery, and uptime values show whether a link is noisy, unstable, or failing.
Environmental and identity fields make measurements traceable to real hardware.
The link-health score uses the weakest category to avoid hiding a failing field.

source_refs.section: Module Telemetry Data and Rating Score Formula

slides.title: Check Your Model: Metrics and Scores
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: Why does the link-health score use the weakest category rather than an average?

choices.text: A single bad category can make a link unreliable even when other categories are healthy.
Averages are impossible to calculate from telemetry fields.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Averages can be calculated, but they can hide the field that should drive action.

choices.text: Only optical power matters for link health.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Optical power is useful, but the model also considers electrical quality, errors, recovery behavior, and context.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The score is designed to preserve the action signal from the weakest category.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the score model: summarization should not mask a failing category.

questions.prompt: Which items are useful telemetry evidence for diagnosing link health?

choices.text: SNR, eye height, PAM4 LTP, and MPI estimation.
Pre-FEC BER, FEC histograms, relinks, LOS, and LOL.
Module temperature, laser temperature, received optical power, and serial identity.
Only the final pass/fail result from an external dashboard.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Useful diagnosis combines quality, error, recovery, environment, and identity context.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Use the category model: link health needs quality, errors, recovery, environment, and identity evidence, not only a final dashboard result.

source_refs.section: Module Telemetry Data and Rating Score Formula

slides.title: Up Next: CDB and Event Workflows
slides.next_module_title: CDB Workflows, Event Logs, and Remote Access
slides.thank_you_message: Nice work - you have finished the telemetry and link-health module.

modules.next_module_title: CDB Workflows, Event Logs, and Remote Access

modules.title: CDB Workflows, Event Logs, and Remote Access
modules.subtitle: The command-reply pattern that moves telemetry, event history, and far-end data.

slides.title: CDB Workflows, Event Logs, and Remote Access
slides.subtitle: How telemetry requests, replies, logs, and far-end access move through the module interface.

slides.title: Workflows You Will Trace
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to follow command, log, and remote-access behavior.
objectives: Trace the four-step CDB command-reply workflow.
Describe why Page 9Fh and extended payload areas matter without memorizing byte maps.
Explain how non-volatile event logs preserve a failure story.
Distinguish remote byte access from remote page access conceptually.

source_refs.section: Transceiver CDB Implementation and Event Log Management

slides.title: CDB Is a Command-Reply Workflow

figure.alt: Silent animation showing a four-step CDB workflow from command preparation through reply retrieval.
figure.caption: A typical CDB command sequence prepares the message, triggers execution, polls status, and retrieves the reply after completion.

slides.title: Page 9Fh Carries the Control Conversation
slides.subtitle: The control page holds command and reply structures; the course emphasizes the pattern rather than every byte field.

bullets.text: The host writes the command identity and related request information.
The module returns completion and response metadata after execution.
Smaller messages can use the local payload area before extended pages are needed.

figure.alt: Page 9Fh simplified structure with command header, reply header, and local payload.
figure.caption: Page 9Fh is the CDB messaging page between host and module in the spec.

slides.title: Extended Payload Handles Larger Transfers
slides.subtitle: When the local payload is not enough, banked payload pages carry larger replies.

bullets.text: Small commands and replies can use the local payload space.
Banked pages A0h through AFh provide larger payload capacity in the spec.
Payload length and check fields help software know what it should retrieve and validate.

figure.alt: Simplified extended payload diagram showing local payload and A0h to AFh pages.
figure.caption: The spec describes extended payload pages for larger command data, up to 2048 bytes.

slides.title: Status Registers Tell the Host When to Read
slides.subtitle: Completion and failure status are part of the workflow, not optional cleanup.

bullets.text: The host should expect command execution to take time and poll progress.
Completion status tells software when the reply can be read.
A failed command must be handled explicitly rather than interpreted as valid telemetry.
Consistent status behavior lets SDKs and command-line tools become reliable operator workflows.

slides.title: Telemetry Retrieval Follows the Same Pattern

figure.alt: Host requests telemetry, local module collects local or remote data, and reply returns health fields.
figure.caption: Telemetry retrieval uses the same command, status, and reply pattern whether data is local or remote.

slides.title: Event Logs Preserve the Failure Story
slides.subtitle: Non-volatile event logs let operators inspect what happened, not only what the link looks like now.

bullets.text: The module records critical events in memory that survives normal power and service workflows.
A unique sequence value helps reconstruct event order, including wraparound behavior.
Each entry carries time and event type so symptoms can be correlated with host and fabric data.
Host APIs should make recent events readable instead of forcing bit-field interpretation during an incident.

figure.alt: Example event log parsing screenshot from the spec document.
figure.caption: The spec includes a parsed event-log example; learner-facing workflows should preserve event order, time, and event meaning.

slides.title: Hysteresis Prevents Noise From Burying Signal
slides.subtitle: Repeated threshold crossings need control so logs remain useful during unstable periods.

cards.title: Threshold events
cards.body: Events can be recorded for link-up, link-down, LOS, LOL, temperature, Rx power, Pre-FEC BER, FEC bins, and more.

cards.title: Hysteresis window
cards.body: The spec lists a default 10-second event hysteresis value for API control.

cards.title: Less duplicate noise
cards.body: A window helps prevent repeated link transitions from overwhelming the log.

cards.title: Programmable behavior
cards.body: Host commands can read or write event hysteresis values for local and remote endpoints.

cards.title: Clearer timeline
cards.body: Operators can focus on distinct events rather than dozens of repeated threshold entries.

cards.title: Version check
cards.body: Final production values should be verified against the published specification revision.

source_refs.section: Event Log IDs, Types, Thresholds, and Host Software APIs

slides.title: Remote CMIS Access Reaches the Far Endpoint
slides.subtitle: Remote access lets host software retrieve information from the partner module through the local compliant module.

bullets.text: Use a targeted request when software needs a small field or narrow range from the remote endpoint.
Use a page-oriented request when software needs a larger CMIS page snapshot.
The host still communicates with the local module; the local module retrieves remote data in-band.

figure.alt: Remote CMIS byte access and page access comparison.
figure.caption: Remote byte and page access differ in size and use case, but both preserve the local-host to local-module control model.

slides.title: CDB Turns Telemetry Into an Operational Workflow
items: CDB uses a repeatable prepare, trigger, poll, and retrieve sequence.
Page 9Fh and payload areas support command and reply exchange; byte maps remain in the spec.
Status checking protects operators from treating unfinished or failed commands as valid data.
Event logs preserve ordered, timestamped evidence after transient failures.
Remote CMIS access lets the host inspect the far endpoint through the local compliant module.

source_refs.section: Transceiver CDB Implementation through Remote CMIS Access

slides.title: Check Your Model: CDB and Events
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best describes a typical CDB interaction?

choices.text: Prepare the command, trigger it, poll status, retrieve the reply.
Retrieve the reply, trigger the command, prepare the header, ignore status.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The reply is read after command completion, and status polling is part of the workflow.

choices.text: Poll status once at boot, then read all telemetry without commands.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Status is checked for each command workflow. Telemetry retrieval still uses command and reply behavior.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. That four-step sequence is the core CDB workflow.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the command pattern: prepare, trigger, poll, retrieve.

questions.prompt: Which features help diagnose a link flap after the immediate symptom has passed?

choices.text: Non-volatile event logs with sequence and timestamps.
Remote CMIS access to inspect the far endpoint.
Status-checked telemetry retrieval rather than reading stale replies.
Removing event hysteresis so every duplicate threshold crossing is always logged.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Durable logs, remote visibility, and disciplined command status all help preserve evidence.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Think evidence path: preserve event history, reach the far endpoint, and verify command completion before trusting the reply.

source_refs.section: Transceiver CDB Implementation and CDB Event Log Management

modules.next_module_title: Firmware, Host Software, and Deployment Readiness

modules.title: Firmware, Host Software, and Deployment Readiness
modules.subtitle: Lifecycle operations and host tooling that turn telemetry into fleet reliability.

slides.title: Firmware, Host Software, and Deployment Readiness
slides.subtitle: How telemetry, remote access, firmware management, and host APIs become fleet-scale operations.

source_refs.section: Firmware Management Features and Host Software

slides.title: Operations You Will Connect
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to explain how lifecycle and host tooling support reliability.
objectives: Describe local and remote firmware management at the capability level.
Explain why host software communicates with the local module even when remote data is requested.
Identify command-line API categories that support operator workflows.
Connect telemetry harvesting, event history, and server aggregation into a deployment-readiness model.

source_refs.section: Firmware Management Features and Host Software

slides.title: Firmware Management Supports Local and Remote Optics
slides.subtitle: The spec includes both CMIS-defined firmware flow and remote vendor-defined upgrade capability.

bullets.text: CMIS-defined firmware management covers host-controlled image download, activation, and status monitoring.
Vendor-defined commands in the spec enable remote firmware upgrades through in-band communication.
Software should determine which firmware management features are available before beginning an update.

figure.alt: Firmware upgrade flow diagram from the spec document.
figure.caption: The source figure shows the CMIS firmware upgrade flow; the course treats it as a lifecycle pattern rather than a memorization target.

slides.title: Hitless Update Is a Lifecycle Pattern

figure.alt: Firmware block download flow diagram from the spec document.
figure.caption: The block-download flow reinforces the idea that firmware update is a stateful lifecycle with checks, retries, completion, and commit behavior.

slides.title: Host Software Owns the Local Conversation
slides.subtitle: The host communicates over I2C with the local compliant module; the local module retrieves remote data.

bullets.text: All compliant transceiver communication with the local host is performed over I2C in the spec.
When remote data is requested, the local transceiver retrieves it and presents it to the host.
The local host does not directly communicate with remote partner modules.

figure.alt: Host communicates with local module over I2C while local module retrieves remote partner data in-band.
figure.caption: The host remains local; the compliant module bridges the remote endpoint.

slides.title: Command-Line APIs Become Operator Tools
slides.subtitle: The host SDK and command-line APIs expose the common module capabilities as repeatable workflows.
rows: Read local registers and standard module state.
Extract standard CMIS data from a remote link partner.
Display local or remote module telemetry parameters.
Read, download, clear, and tune local or remote event history.
Run local or remote hitless firmware update workflows.
Set epoch time, adjust hysteresis, and display module link rating.

slides.title: Telemetry Harvesting Builds a Link Timeline

figure.alt: Timeline showing host polling, event threshold, remote partner query, and fabric health map.
figure.caption: Continuous harvesting can turn current telemetry, event logs, and remote queries into a fabric-wide health timeline.

slides.title: Deployment Readiness Checks
slides.subtitle: A deployment should verify behavior, not only whether fields exist.

cards.title: Local and remote partners
cards.body: Confirm the host can retrieve data from local modules and the correct remote endpoints.

cards.title: Breakout cases
cards.body: Verify partner identity and per-endpoint telemetry when one port maps to multiple remote sides.

cards.title: Third-party optics
cards.body: Check behavior when compliant and non-compliant optics appear in realistic environments.

cards.title: Epoch time
cards.body: Ensure timestamps can be correlated across host, module, event logs, and monitoring tools.

cards.title: Firmware lifecycle
cards.body: Exercise discovery, transfer, activation, commit, abort, and recovery states.

cards.title: Repository and SDK
cards.body: Confirm the host implementation, tools, and source availability expected by deployment policy.

source_refs.section: Other MCU Requirements, Host Software, Requirements and Repository

slides.title: From Link Flap to Root Cause

figure.alt: Silent animation showing troubleshooting from a link flap alert through health score, metrics, event log, remote query, and evidence-based action.
figure.caption: A practical workflow combines score, metrics, event history, remote endpoint data, and host timeline before choosing an action.

slides.title: Reliability Depends on Common Interfaces
items: Firmware management is a lifecycle with capability discovery, status checks, activation, commit, and recovery behavior.
Host software talks locally over I2C while compliant modules retrieve remote partner data in-band.
Operator APIs should expose telemetry, event logs, remote access, firmware update, time, hysteresis, and link rating.
Continuous harvesting turns current state and event history into a link timeline.
Deployment readiness means verifying behavior across local, remote, breakout, and mixed-compatibility cases.

source_refs.section: Firmware Management Features and Host Software

slides.title: Check Your Model: Deployment Workflow
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: Why does the local host not directly communicate with remote partner modules in the spec model?

choices.text: The host communicates with the local compliant module; the local module retrieves remote data in-band.
Remote telemetry is not supported.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Remote telemetry is supported through the local module and in-band communication.

choices.text: Only the telemetry server can ask for remote optical data.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Host software can request remote data through the local compliant module; the server aggregates higher-level health information.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The local module bridges remote retrieval while the host keeps one local control path.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the host boundary: local host to local module over I2C, then local module to remote partner in-band.

questions.prompt: Which host/API features support fleet-scale optics operations?

choices.text: Local and remote telemetry download.
Event-log read, clear, recent-event download, and hysteresis control.
Local and remote firmware update workflows.
Ignoring host-side pre-FEC BER and uncorrectable codeword data.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Fleet operations need current telemetry, durable history, remote access, firmware lifecycle support, and host-side context.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Think about the full operations chain: current telemetry, event history, remote access, firmware management, time correlation, rating, and host-side context.

source_refs.section: Transceiver Communications and Command Line APIs

slides.title: Course Complete: Enhanced Telemetry Optics
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing the final module: Firmware, Host Software, and Deployment Readiness.
slides.cert_message: You have completed the optics reliability course. You can now explain how enhanced telemetry connects local and remote endpoints, link-health metrics, event history, CDB workflows, firmware lifecycle, and host APIs into an evidence-driven reliability model.
